title: Share of land defined as natural habitat
subtitle: Natural ecosystems include forest, grassland, savanna, and shrublands, and other classes such as peatlands and marshes. It is total land minus land that is used by humans for agriculture – either croplands or pasture – or for infrastructure and urban environments.
caption:
Share of land defined as natural habitat
Natural ecosystems include forest, grassland, savanna, and shrublands, and other classes such as peatlands and marshes. It is total land minus land that is used by humans for agriculture – either croplands or pasture – or for infrastructure and urban environments.

This line chart tracks the percentage of total land area classified as natural habitat across four entities from 1780 to 2010. The vertical axis measures the share of land from 0% to 80%, while the horizontal axis spans over two centuries. The green line representing the World average begins as the highest value, starting near 75% in 1780 and following a consistent downward trajectory to finish just under 50% by 2010. In contrast, the lines for Germany, France, and the United Kingdom display a distinct U-shaped pattern. The United Kingdom, shown in brown, starts at approximately 64% in 1780, declines steadily to a minimum near 29% around 1940, and then rises to roughly 48% by the end of the period. Germany and France follow similar paths; both begin between 46% and 49%, dip to lows between 40% and 46% in the early 20th century, and then climb sharply after 1950. By 2010, Germany reaches the highest point among the specific countries at roughly 65%, with France close behind at 62%. The data shows that while the global share of natural habitat has decreased over time, these three European nations experienced a period of intense land conversion followed by a substantial recovery in natural habitat share during the latter half of the 20th century.
